items.question: Who relies on Matplotlib in a typical ML Python stack?
items.answer: Data scientists, ML engineers, and researchers use Matplotlib directly for EDA plots and indirectly through pandas, scikit-learn display helpers, and Seaborn—all of which ultimately draw via Matplotlib artists.

items.question: What is the difference between a Figure and an Axes in Matplotlib?
items.answer: A Figure is the top-level canvas that can contain one or more Axes subplots; an Axes is a single plot region where you call scatter, plot, hist, bar, and set axis labels, title, and legend.

items.question: When should you prefer ax.scatter with alpha over opaque points?
items.answer: When many observations overlap in feature or coordinate space—common in ML datasets with thousands of rows—alpha transparency reveals density structure that a solid scatter would hide.

items.question: Where does Matplotlib sit relative to Seaborn and embedding visualization?
items.answer: Matplotlib owns the pyplot API and Figure/Axes drawing primitives; Seaborn wraps statistical plots on top of it, and embedding visualization owns interpretation practices for coordinate scatter plots often drawn with ax.scatter.

items.question: Why use object-oriented fig, ax = plt.subplots() instead of bare plt.plot in reusable code?
items.answer: OO style makes multi-panel figures explicit, avoids pyplot global state bugs when functions create multiple plots, and returns Figure objects you can save or pass to callers without side effects.

items.question: How do you save a notebook figure for a report without clipped labels?
items.answer: Call fig.tight_layout() then fig.savefig('name.png', dpi=150, bbox_inches='tight') on the Figure object; set figsize before plotting so text remains readable at the target resolution.

items.question: pyplot state interface vs object-oriented Axes methods: when to use each?
items.answer: Use pyplot (plt.plot, plt.xlabel) for quick one-cell notebook exploration. Use fig, ax = plt.subplots() and ax methods when building functions, dashboards, or multi-subplot grids—OO avoids implicit current-axes confusion.

items.question: Matplotlib ax.hist vs pandas df.hist: what is the trade-off?
items.answer: pandas df.hist is faster for a matrix of column histograms with sensible defaults. ax.hist gives per-axes control over bins, colors, and layout in a custom subplot grid—prefer Matplotlib OO when you need fine-grained styling or non-DataFrame arrays.

items.question: Cause: plt.plot called repeatedly in a loop without label= or legend(). Effect?
items.answer: Multiple series render indistinguishably on the same axes with no legend entries—viewers cannot tell train from validation loss or class A from class B, undermining EDA and training-curve monitoring.

items.question: Cause: savefig called before tight_layout on a figure with long y-axis tick labels. Effect?
items.answer: Exported PNG or PDF clips axis titles and tick text at the figure edge, producing unusable report figures that require manual cropping or regeneration.
